coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical and photosensing study of nitrogen doped carbon nanoparticles sensitized TiO2 nanorods
coredata.dc:creator: Debnath, Kamalesh
Majumder, Tanmoy
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped Carbon Nanoparticles (NCNPs) were synthesized using a thermal pyrolysis method. NCNPs are crystalline in nature with diameter ranges from size 9–36 nm. Optical absorption study revealed two major peaks. The sharp peak at 220 nm is attributed to π → π* transition in aromatic sp2 CC bonds and the broad peak at 300–400 nm corresponds to n → π* transition in C=O/C=N bonds. NCNPs are highly luminescent and demonstrated excitation dependent green photoemission under 340-500 nm photoexcitation. TiO2 nanorods (TiO2 NRs) were grown on Fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates using hydrothermal method. Photoelectrochemical properties of pristine TiO2 NRs and NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs has been studied in detail. NCNPs sensitized nanorods demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency (η) of 1.37% and incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 80% at 340 nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanorods
Carbon nanoparticles
Photoelectrochemical
Photoconversion efficiency
Incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCNPs and attachment with TiO2 NRs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge Central Research facility (CRF), NIT Agartala for the SEM & XRD characterization; DST FIST project of Department of Physics, NIT Agartala for UV–visible absorption spectroscopy measurements, Department of Chemistry, NIT Agartala for PL measurements; Institute Instrumentation Centre, IIT Roorkee for XPS measurements; SAIF, NEHU for TEM characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top view SEM image of TiO2 nanorods, (b) magnified SEM image of TiO2 nanorods and (c) Cross-sectional view of TiO2 nanorods.
X-ray diffraction spectrum of (a) TiO2 NRs on FTO substrate and (b) bare FTO substrate.
Typical UV–visible absorption spectrum of TiO2 NRs. Tauc's plot of TiO2 NRs is shown in inset.

(a) TEM image of NCNPs. (b) Lateral size distribution histogram estimated from N = 45 particles; red solid line is the Gaussian fitting curve (c) high resolution TEM image of a typical NCNP. (d-f) Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) patterns, auto-correlated HRTEM lattice image and line profile analysis of the diffraction fringes respectively of the selected area (red square in panel c); the lattice parameter revealed is 0.21 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS full scan spectrum of NCNPs. High resolution XPS peaks of N1s electronic state of NCNPs is presented at inset.

(a) Typical UV–visible absorption spectrum of NCNPs, (b) normalized PL spectra of NCNPs at different excitation wavelengths and (c) digital photograph of NCNPs solution under illumination of various wavelength of light.
XPS full scan spectrum of NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs.
(a) SEM image of NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs. (b) EDX spectrum of NCNPs/TiO2 NRs. (c) Chemical mapping of the key elements in NCNPs/TiO2 NRs.

(a) Linear sweep voltammograms of pristine TiO2 and NCNP/TiO2 nanorods recorded under white light illumination (100 mW/cm2). (b) Transient photoresponse curve of pristine TiO2 and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods. (c) Photoconversion efficiency vs bias voltage plots of pristine TiO2 and NCNP/TiO2 nanorods. (d) IPCE (%) vs. wavelength plots of pristine TiO2 and NCNP/TiO2 nanorods at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl.

Nyquist plots [Re(Z) vs. Im(Z)] of TiO2 and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorod photoelectrodes under white light illumination (100 mW/cm2). (b) RC Equivalent circuit TiO2 NRs/electrolyte junction. (c) Schematic representation of charge transport mechanism under illumination of light.
Photocurrent retention behavior of NCNPs/TiO2 NRs at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl under white light illumination (100 mW/cm2).
Fitted parameters from the Nyquist plots of TiO2 and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods photoanodes under white light (100 mW/cm2) illumination.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical and photosensing study of nitrogen doped carbon nanoparticles sensitized TiO2 nanorods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, India -799046

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, India -799046

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, Tripura Institute of Technology, Agartala 799015, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, Tripura Institute of Technology, Agartala, India -799015

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped Carbon Nanoparticles (NCNPs) were synthesized using a thermal pyrolysis method. NCNPs are crystalline in nature with diameter ranges from size 9–36 nm. Optical absorption study revealed two major peaks. The sharp peak at 220 nm is attributed to π → π* transition in aromatic sp2 CC bonds and the broad peak at 300–400 nm corresponds to n → π* transition in C=O/C=N bonds. NCNPs are highly luminescent and demonstrated excitation dependent green photoemission under 340-500 nm photoexcitation. TiO2 nanorods (TiO2 NRs) were grown on Fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates using hydrothermal method. Photoelectrochemical properties of pristine TiO2 NRs and NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs has been studied in detail. NCNPs sensitized nanorods demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency (η) of 1.37% and incident-photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 80% at 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) solar energy conversion is an attractive area of research to produce green energy by virtue of their low cost and easy fabrication process compared to the conventional Si based solar cells [1–3]. Among the different photoanode materials, TiO2 is one of the promising candidates owing to its high photoresponsivity, better photocorrosion stability, appropriate band position for water splitting, low cost, easy synthesis process, non-toxic and eco-friendly nature [3–8]. However, low visible photo-absorption of TiO2 due to its large bandgap restricts the further enhancement of solar energy conversion. To overcome this problem, several techniques have been established to improve the photoabsorption from visible to infrared region, a popular one out of which is sensitizing the TiO2 with narrow bandgap semiconductors such as PbS, CdS, CdSeS, PbSe, CdSe and CdTe etc. [3,9–13]. Although, such chalcogenide semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) exhibits high power conversion efficiency, the major bottleneck for their practical application is their toxicity and instability in aqueous solution under light illumination. Recently, carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon dots (CDs), have been studied as a green photosensitizers functionalized with ZnO, TiO2, α-Fe2O3, Cu2S, SrTiO3 and BiVO4 nanostructures for photodetectors [14], photoelectrochemical cells [15–21], solar cells [22,23] and photocatalytic applications [24]. Carbon based nanomaterials have also demonstrated good performance including broad photoabsorption, size dependent photoluminescence properties, nontoxicity, chemical inertness and large scale production capability [25–29]. Optical absorption, emission and electrical conductivity of carbon nanomaterials can be enhanced by doping with nitrogen and others elements like S, B, Si, F, K [30–34]. Zhang et al. studied photocatalytic properties of N doped carbon dot decorated TiO2 inverse opal [35]. The photocatalytic efficiency of N doped carbon dot decorated TiO2 inverse opal was significantly increased because of the enhancement of visible photon absorption. Liang et al. observed six times photocurrent enhancement in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized hydrogenated TiO2 nanostructures in comparison to the control samples [36]. Zhou et al. reported CQDs/TiO2 based heterojunction photoanode which significantly minimized the electron-hole recombination and enhanced the photoelectrochemical properties [37].

In this report, we have studied the growth and characteristics of N doped carbon nanoparticles sensitized TiO2 nanorods (NCNPs/TiO2 NRs). The photoelectrochemical properties of NCNPs/TiO2 NRs were also studied in details.

TiO2 NRs were grown hydrothermally on Fluorine doped SnO2 (FTO) coated glass substrates. At first, 12 ml concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) (37% by weight) was added drop wise to 18 ml of DI water. Next, 400 μl of titanium (IV) butaoxide (Ti(C4H9O)4) was added to the previous solution (30 ml) followed by magnetic stirring until a clear precursor solution was achieved. The cleaned FTO glass substrate was placed in a 25 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave filled with the above precursor solution. The hydrothermal growth was carried out at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h in an electric oven. When the temperature of oven decreased to below 50 °C, the autoclave was removed and further cooled down under flowing tap water. The TiO2 deposited FTO substrates was cleaned with DI water and dried with hot air under ambient condition. A visibly uniform white colored film was observed on the conducting side of the FTO, which ensured the growth of good quality TiO2 NRs. To achieve better crystallinity the as prepared TiO2 NRs were annealed at 500 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCNPs and attachment with TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: N doped carbon nanoparticles (NCNPs) were prepared using thermal pyrolysis technique. At first, 1 g powder of citric acid and 1 g of urea were mixed together using a mortar pestle. The prepared powder was then transferred in a Teflon-lined Stainless Steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. When the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, a black colored paste was obtained which confirmed the growth of N doped CNPs. The as grown NCNPs were dispersed into 100 ml of DI water for further use. TiO2 NRs samples grown on FTO substrates was sensitized by dipping them in the NCNPs solution for different times (30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h and 5 h) to obtain NCNPs/TiO2 NRs. The NCNPs/TiO2 NRs are then finally baked at 120 °C on a hot plate for 3 h to obtain samples for electrochemical characterizations.

The surface morphology and cross-sectional image of TiO2 NRs were obtained using a FEI, Nova Nano SEM 450 Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study of NCNPs was performed using a JEOL, JEM-2100, 200 kV electron microscope. Crystalline phase of the nanorods was studied using Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer with CuKα (λ = 1.54058 Å) radiation. Chemical bonding of NCNPs and TiO2 NRs were investigated using PHI 5000 Versa Probe III X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). UV–visible absorption study was done using PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. Excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra were determined using Perkin Elmer (Model LS55) spectrofluorometer. Electrochemical study was carried out using CHI 660D, USA electrochemical workstation using a three electrode setup. NCNPs/TiO2 NRs were used as working electrodes (photoanodes), platinum wire was used as counter electrode and a calibrated Ag/AgCl was taken as reference electrode. All the photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were performed in a 1 M KOH electrolyte. A 300 W Xenon light source (Sciencetech, Canada) was used to illuminate the photoanodes with broadband light of intensity ~100 mW/cm2. The incident-photon-to-current-conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were conducted with various wavelength of light from a monochormator (Sciencetech, Canada) coupled with the Xenon Light source. The intensities of the light of various wavelengths were measured using a calibrated photodiode and power meter.

Fig. 1(a) shows the top view SEM micrograph of hydrothermally grown TiO2 NRs on FTO substrates. The SEM image clearly indicates the nearly vertically aligned, uniform and dense growth of TiO2 NRs distributed all over the FTO substrate. The nanorod density calculated from SEM micrographs is 1.2 × 109/cm2. The magnified SEM image of TiO2 NRs is presented in Fig. 1(b). The micrograph clearly shows the formation of nanorods with tetragonal in shape having a square top surface, which was also observed by other researchers for hydrothermally grown TiO2 nanostructures [38,39]. The average diameter of the nanorods is about 170 nm. The cross-section image of the sample is presented in Fig. 1(c) from which the average length of nanorods is estimated to be 2 μm. The XRD pattern of TiO2 nanorods and FTO substrate are presented in Fig. 2 . The diffraction pattern clearly shows that nanorods are highly crystalline. The XRD peaks at 2θ ~ 37.77° and 62.10° corresponds to the diffraction from (101) and (002) planes of rutile TiO2 NRs, respectively (JCPDS No.88-1175) [38,40]. The band gap of the nanorods was calculated from absorption spectra as depicted in Fig. 3 . The band gap of TiO2 NRs was calculated using the Tauc's formula [41].
(1)
αϑh
1 n
= A
hϑ −
E g
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the incident photon energy, A is a constant which depends on the refractive index and electron/hole masses. The value of n depends on the direct and indirect transitions of electrons. For rutile TiO2 NRs, the value of n = 1/2 for direct allowed transitions. The inset of Fig. 3 shows Tauc's plot of nanorods. The band gap of TiO2 has been determined by extrapolating the linear portion of the (αhν)2 vs (hν) graph to the energy axis (hν) (inset of Fig. 3). The calculated band gap is found to be 3.18 eV, which corresponds to the bulk band gap value of TiO2 (3–3.2 eV) [42].

Microstructural study of N doped carbon nanoparticles (NCNPs) was performed using TEM analysis. The plane view TEM image (Fig. 4(a)) revealed that, the NCNPs are quasi spherical in shape and well dispersed without any visible agglomeration. The histogram in Fig. 4(b) obtained by counting 45 nanoparticles, shows Gaussian distribution of the particles with diameter ranging from 9 to 36 nm. The average size of the particles has been estimated to be 18 nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a typical NCNP is presented in Fig. 4(c). The presence of lattice fringes in HRTEM image confirmed the crystalline nature of NCNPs. Fig. 4(d), (e) and (f) show the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) patterns, auto-correlated HRTEM lattice image & line profile analysis of lattice fringes obtained from HRTEM image, respectively. The lattice spacing has been estimated from TEM study and found to be 0.21 nm which corresponds to (100) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [43–45].

Fig. 5 shows the full range XPS spectrum of N doped carbon nanoparticles, which confirmed the presence of the elements N, O and C. The binding energy peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to C1s electrons. On the other hand, the peak at 399.6 eV is ascribed to the presence of nitrogen within the carbon matrix [16,46]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s peak is presented at the inset (top left) of Fig. 5. The 1s peak of N in NCNP has been deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.5 eV, which corresponds to pyrrole-like N atoms and graphite-like atoms, respectively [46,47]. The atomic percentage of the elements N, C and O in NCNPs has been obtained from XPS peaks and shown at the inset of Fig. 5. The atomic percentage of the doped N atom in the NCNPs was found to be 6.71%.

Fig. 6(a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCNPs recorded in the wavelength range 200–800 nm. In the absorption spectrum, two distinct peaks, a sharp peak at 220 nm and another broad peak at 300–400 nm were observed. The strong UV absorption at 220 nm is attributed to π → π* transition in aromatic sp2 CC bonds which often observed in carbon nanoparticles [16,47]. On the other hand, the broad peak at 300–400 nm corresponds to n → π* transition in C=O/C=N bonds [16,48]. The excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra of NCNPs are shown in Fig. 6(b). As the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 500 nm, the emission peaks shifted from 533 ‐ to 557 nm, respectively. Similar excitation dependent PL emission often observed in fluorescence carbon nanomaterials due to the formation of surface states [49,50]. More importantly, such strong green emission may be attributed to the presence of carboxyl and carbonyl groups in carbon backbone of NCNPs [51]. The digital photograph of the samples under sunlight and under light of wavelength 420, 440 and 460 nm is shown in Fig. 6(c). Interestingly, excellent visible green emission has been observed under illumination of 420, 440 and 460 nm radiation.

To confirm the attachment of NCNPs on TiO2 nanorods, XPS study of NCNPs deposited on TiO2 NRs was performed. Fig. 7 represents full-range XPS spectrum of NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs. The spectrum clearly shows the presence of the elements Ti, C, O and N in the nanorods originating from NCNPs and TiO2 NRs. The binding energy peaks at 37.39 eV, 61.10 eV, 455.76 eV and 564.29 eV are assigned to Ti 3p, Ti 3s, Ti 2p and Ti 2s electrons of TiO2, respectively [52]. The peak at 399.6 eV represents 1s peak of N in NCNPs. The High resolution XPS of N 1s peak (inset of Fig. 7) in the NCNPs/TiO2 NRs spectrum has been deconvoluted in two pronounced Gaussian peaks at 399.9 and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyrrole-like N atoms and graphite-like N atoms, respectively. These peaks are similar to N 1 s peaks of NCNP in Fig. 5 and confirmed the successful attachment of NCNPs onto TiO2 NRs surface [16,19].

Fig. 8(a) shows a typical SEM image of TiO2 NRs after attachment of NCNPs. No morphological changes were observed for NCNPs-decorated TiO2 because of the very small size of the particle. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) study in Fig. 8(b) showed the presence of elements Ti, O, Sn, N, and C. These results are consistent with the XPS analysis and further confirmed the attachment of NCNPs on TiO2 NRs surfaces. Also, existence of nitrogen and carbon in chemical mapping study in Fig. 8(c) demonstrated the successful attachment of NCNPs on TiO2 nanorods surface.

Photoelectrochemical behaviors of TiO2 NRs and NCNPs decorated TiO2 NRs were studied under dark and illumination of white light from a broadband source. Fig. 9(a) represents the linear sweep voltammograms of pristine TiO2 NRs and NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs with different attachment times. It has been observed that, TiO2 NRs shows very small dark current (J ~ 10
−7 A/cm2 at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl), but upon illumination of white light of intensity 100 mW/cm2, the observed photocurrent density was increased significantly to 1.80 mA/cm2 at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl. More importantly, the photocurrent has been significantly improved after attachment of NCNPs. The maximum photocurrent was observed for 2 h attachment of NCNPs and found to be 3.0 mA/cm2 at 0.5V vs Ag/AgCl, which is ~1.7 times higher than pristine TiO2 NRs. Fig. 9(b) shows the transient photoresponse curve (J–t) of pristine TiO2 NRs and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorod at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under illumination of white light with intensity of 100 mW/cm2 at different attachment time. The highest short-circuit current (J
SC ) was observed for 2 h attachment of NCNPs and equals to 2.8 mA/cm2. It is evident that the J
SC value of NCNPs sensitized nanorods is 1.7 times higher than pristine TiO2 NRs. Such enhancement of photocurrent is attributed to the higher photoabsorption and better carrier transport in NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods. However, at much higher attachment times such as 3 h, and 5 h, the short-circuit current density decreases owing to the deposition of a thick layer of NCNPs [14,16]. The photoconversion efficiency (i.e., photon to hydrogen) of pristine TiO2 NR and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorod samples was calculated using the following equation [14,21].
(2)
η
%
=
J ph
1.23 −
V applied
P in
× 100
where η is photoconversion efficiency, J
ph is photocurrent density (mA/cm2), Vapplied is the applied potential vs Ag/AgCl, and Pin is the input power of the incident light (100 mW/cm2). Fig. 9(c) represents the plot of photoconversion efficiency of the samples against the applied potential. The maximum efficiency obtained was 1.37% (at −0.428 V vs. Ag/AgCl) for the NCNPs/TiO2 nanorod sample with an attachment time of 2 h. Conversely, the pristine TiO2 nanorod displayed an efficiency of 0.716% at −0.403 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Our study suggests that, the photoconversion efficiency increases with NCNPs attachment time up to 2 h and beyond it decreases due to thick NCNPs layer formation which corroborates our linear sweep voltammeter as well as transient J–t study [14,21].

To further study the role of NCNPs, incident-photon-to-current-conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were carried out at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl. IPCE of TiO2 NRs and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods were estimated using the following equation [14,16].
(3)
IPCE
%
=
1240
J λ
λ
P λ
× 100
where J λ is photocurrent (mA/cm2), λ is light wavelength (nm) and P
λ is the light intensity (mW/cm2). Fig. 9(d) shows the IPCE vs λ plots of pristine TiO2 NRs and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods (loading time 2 h). Interestingly, NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs shows significant enhancement of IPCE compared to pristine nanorods and the maximum value is 80% at wavelength 340 nm. The remarkable enhancement of IPCE is attributed to the better photoabsorption in UV region and lower charge transfer resistance at TiO2 NRs/NCNPs interface.

The charge transfer process at photoanode-electrolyte interface is studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements under dark and white light illumination of 100 mW/cm2. Fig. 10(a) shows the electrochemical impedance plots (Im Z vs Real Z plots) of TiO2 NRs and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods under white light illumination. The experimental EIS results were fitted with an equivalent circuit model consisting of two RC parallel circuits and a series resistance [20]. The schematic representation of equivalent circuit diagram is shown in Fig. 10(b). In the equivalent circuit, the parameters RS, RSC and Rct represent the series resistance, semiconductor resistance and charge transfer resistance at semiconductor/electrolyte junction, respectively. The capacitances CH and Cdl are known as Helmholtz double layer capacitance and depletion capacitance, respectively. All the extracted values of EIS parameters from experimental data under light illumination are shown in Table 1 . Under illumination, charge transfer resistances are reduced significantly due to the generation of electron-hole pairs within TiO2. The Rct values of TiO2 NRs and NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods under illumination are 775 Ω and 434 Ω, respectively. A schematic of the charge transfer mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 10(c). Decrease of charge transfer resistance of NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods is attributed to the favorable band alignment with nitrogen doped carbon nanoparticles.

To study the stability of NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods, photocurrent measurements with respect to time was carried out at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under 100 mW/cm2 white light illumination. Fig. 11 shows the variation of photocurrent with time for NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods. Interestingly, after 120 mins of measurement under white light illumination, no noticeable photocurrent decay was detected. In a typical photoelectrochemical process, photogenerated holes in the valance band of n-type working electrode oxidize H2O to O2 in the electrolyte, whereas electrons in the conduction band are migrated to the counter electrode and reduce H+ ions to H2. As photogenerated holes in TiO2 NRs have relatively slower interfacial charge transfer kinetics, therefore, accumulated holes in nanorods promote hole induce anodic corrosion [53]. The improved electrochemical stability in NCNPs/TiO2 nanorods is attributed to the fast and easy holes transfer from TiO2 NRs to electrolyte due to favorable band alignment.

In summary, we have studied PEC and photosensing nature of N doped carbon nanoparticles decorated TiO2 NRs. TiO2 NRs were grown by hydrothermal process on FTO substrates. XRD study revealed that TiO2 NRs are highly crystalline with rutile phase. The optical band gap was calculated from UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and found to be 3.18 eV. N doped carbon nanoparticles were grown by a thermal pyrolysis process form a mixture of citric acid and urea. TEM study revealed that carbon nanoparticles are highly crystalline with an average diameter of 18 nm. The chemical bonding and N doping in carbon network of NCNPs was investigated using XPS measurements. The optical spectrum of NCNPs showed a strong UV absorption peak at 220 nm and broad absorption peak at around 331 nm. NCNPs are highly luminescent and demonstrated excitation dependent photoemission in visible spectrum. To improve the PEC activities, NCNPs were attached with TiO2 NRs. NCNPs sensitized TiO2 NRs demonstrated superior photoconversion efficiency, incident photon to current conversion efficiency and photosensing behavior compared to pristine nanorods.

Kamalesh Debnath: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Collection, Writing- Original draft preparation. Dr. Tanmoy Majumder: Sample Characterization, Experimental help. and Dr. Suvra Prakash Mondal: Supervision, Correction of Manuscript, Academic discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: We acknowledge Central Research facility (CRF), NIT Agartala for the SEM & XRD characterization; DST FIST project of Department of Physics, NIT Agartala for UV–visible absorption spectroscopy measurements, Department of Chemistry, NIT Agartala for PL measurements; Institute Instrumentation Centre, IIT Roorkee for XPS measurements; SAIF, NEHU for TEM characterizations.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical devices for solar water splitting-materials and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 1. C. Jiang, S.J.A. Moniz, A. Wang, T. Zhang, J. Tang, Photoelectrochemical Devices for Solar Water Splitting-Materials and Challenges. Chem. Soc. Rev. 46 (2017) 4645-4660.
2. M.G. Walter, E.L. Warren, J.R. McKone, S.W. Boettcher, Q. Mi, E.A. Santori, N.S. Lewis, Solar Water Splitting Cells, Chem. Rev. 110 (2010) 6446-6473.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Titanium dioxide nanostructures for photoelectrochemical applications

4. A, Kusior, Anna Wnuk, A, T. Zajac, K. Zakrzewska, M. Radecka, TiO2 Nanostructures for Photoelectrochemical Cells (PECs), Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 40 (2015) 4936-4944.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical water splitting using dense and aligned TiO2 nanorod arrays

Dionysiou, A Review on the Visible Light Active Titanium Dioxide Photocatalysts for Environmental Applications, Appl. Catal. B 125 (2012) 331-349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical properties of PbS quantum dot sensitized TiO2 nanorods photoelectrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 9. Y Limin, J. Junhong, Y. Geween, H. Minmin, Photoelectrochemical Properties of PbS Quantum Dot Sensitized TiO2 Nanorods Photoelectrodes, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 33279-33286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnS passivated PbSe sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays to suppress photocorrosion in photoelectrochemical solar cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical properties properties of CdS/CdSe sensitized TiO2 nanocable arrays
